Before Israel enters the promised land, God establishes the rules of war—and they begin not with strategy or weaponry but with a command rooted in memory. "Do not be afraid," the priest declares, "for the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, is with you." This is not naive optimism. Israel will face superior numbers, intimidating chariots, and overwhelming force. The command to be unafraid is grounded in what God has already done. He parted the Red Sea. He defeated Pharaoh. He sustained a nation in the wilderness for forty years. The priest reminds Israel that their confidence cannot rest on military advantage or personal courage—it must rest on the presence and faithfulness of God. This is the only antidote to the terror that threatens to paralyze warriors before battle.

Yet God's law makes room for human limits and legitimate attachments to ordinary life. A man who has built a house deserves to dedicate it himself. A man who planted a vineyard should taste its fruit. A man engaged to be married has a right to that union. Even the fearful are not condemned—they are simply sent home, to protect the army's morale. God does not demand that His people pretend to be unafraid or ignore the weight of what they face. He allows them to acknowledge their true condition and removes those whose fear might spread like disease through the camp. This reveals something hard: faithfulness sometimes means stepping back, accepting limitation, and trusting that God's purposes will be accomplished without you.

The laws governing conquered cities show the stark difference between distant enemies and the inhabitants of Canaan itself. Cities far away may be offered peace and enslaved if they refuse it. But the Canaanite nations—the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, and others—must be completely destroyed. The reason given is not racial superiority or territorial greed but spiritual survival: they will teach Israel to commit the "detestable things" that they practice for their gods, and Israel will sin against the LORD. This is not presented as casual cruelty but as necessary judgment. God had given Israel clear warning at the beginning of this law: these nations will corrupt you unless they are removed. The command is severe, and we are right to feel the weight of it. But we must not soften it into something Scripture does not say, nor can we dismiss it as merely ancient custom. God deemed it necessary, and His word stands.

The law closes with an unexpected detail about siege warfare: spare the fruit trees. Even in the conquest of the promised land, even when facing complete destruction of enemy peoples, Israel is forbidden to burn the orchards. "Are the trees of the field human, that you should besiege them?" This strange question reveals that war itself has limits set by God. Destruction may be commanded, but waste is forbidden. Creation exists for use, not for spite. A warrior can kill an enemy soldier but cannot poison the land he will inherit. This law insists that even holy war serves the purposes of life, not death for its own sake. It is a small mercy in a chapter that contains large horrors—but it is mercy nonetheless, and it comes from the God who commands it all.
